questions.question: Order the steps of how positional encoding is applied to input tokens.
items: Apply dropout to the combined representation
Convert input tokens into embedding vectors
Add the positional encoding matrix to the token embeddings
Compute or look up a positional encoding for each position
Pass the result into the Transformer layers
correct_order: Convert input tokens into embedding vectors
Compute or look up a positional encoding for each position
Add the positional encoding matrix to the token embeddings
Apply dropout to the combined representation
Pass the result into the Transformer layers
questions.explanation: First, tokens are converted to embeddings. Then, a positional encoding vector is computed for each position. These encodings are added to the embeddings so each token gains position information. Dropout is applied to prevent over-reliance on positions. Finally, the combined representation enters the Transformer layers.

questions.question: Order the data flow through an encoder-decoder Transformer from input to output.
items: Decoder generates output tokens one at a time using causal masking
Add positional encodings to input token embeddings
Encoder processes the full input with bidirectional attention
Output probability distribution over the vocabulary for the next token
Decoder uses cross-attention to attend to encoder hidden states
correct_order: Add positional encodings to input token embeddings
Encoder processes the full input with bidirectional attention
Decoder generates output tokens one at a time using causal masking
Decoder uses cross-attention to attend to encoder hidden states
Output probability distribution over the vocabulary for the next token
questions.explanation: The flow begins with adding positional encodings to input embeddings. The encoder processes the full input bidirectionally. The decoder then generates tokens one at a time with causal self-attention, using cross-attention to consult the encoder output. Finally, a probability distribution is produced for the next token.

questions.question: Order the steps of using BERT for a classification task.
items: Output the predicted class label
Process the full input through bidirectional self-attention layers
Feed the [CLS] token representation into a classification layer
Tokenize the input text and prepend [CLS]
Add positional encodings to the token embeddings
correct_order: Tokenize the input text and prepend [CLS]
Add positional encodings to the token embeddings
Process the full input through bidirectional self-attention layers
Feed the [CLS] token representation into a classification layer
Output the predicted class label
questions.explanation: BERT classification works by first tokenizing and adding [CLS], then adding positional encodings, then processing everything through bidirectional attention so [CLS] aggregates the full sequence meaning, then passing [CLS] through a classifier, and finally outputting the predicted label.

questions.question: Order the steps of BERT's masked-language modeling training.
items: Predict the original tokens at the masked positions
Process the modified sequence through the bidirectional encoder
Randomly select tokens to mask
Replace selected tokens with [MASK] or other tokens
Compute the loss between predictions and original tokens
correct_order: Randomly select tokens to mask
Replace selected tokens with [MASK] or other tokens
Process the modified sequence through the bidirectional encoder
Predict the original tokens at the masked positions
Compute the loss between predictions and original tokens
questions.explanation: MLM training starts by selecting tokens to mask, then replacing them (mostly with [MASK]). The corrupted sequence is processed through the encoder with bidirectional attention. The model predicts what the original tokens were at the masked positions, and the loss is computed to update the weights.

questions.question: Order the steps of GPT generating a response.
items: Select the most likely next token from the distribution
Apply softmax to get a probability distribution over the vocabulary
Append the selected token and repeat the process
Feed the current tokens through the decoder layers with causal masking
Start with the input prompt tokens
correct_order: Start with the input prompt tokens
Feed the current tokens through the decoder layers with causal masking
Apply softmax to get a probability distribution over the vocabulary
Select the most likely next token from the distribution
Append the selected token and repeat the process
questions.explanation: GPT generates text by starting with prompt tokens, processing them through decoder layers where each token only attends to past tokens (causal masking), computing a probability distribution over the vocabulary, selecting the next token, and repeating until done.

questions.question: Order the frequency behavior of positional encoding from lowest embedding dimensions to highest.
items: Slow changes capturing sentence-level patterns
Very slow changes capturing document-level patterns
Rapid changes capturing local token differences
Moderate changes capturing phrase-level patterns
correct_order: Rapid changes capturing local token differences
Moderate changes capturing phrase-level patterns
Slow changes capturing sentence-level patterns
Very slow changes capturing document-level patterns
questions.explanation: As the dimension index increases, the frequency decreases. Lower dimensions change rapidly (high frequency) to capture fine-grained local differences between nearby tokens. Higher dimensions change more slowly (low frequency), capturing broader patterns at the phrase, sentence, and document level.

questions.question: Order the steps of cross-attention in an encoder-decoder model.
items: Compute a weighted sum of the values using the attention weights
The decoder creates queries from its hidden states
Apply softmax to get attention weights
The encoder provides keys and values from its hidden states
Compute attention scores between decoder queries and encoder keys
correct_order: The encoder provides keys and values from its hidden states
The decoder creates queries from its hidden states
Compute attention scores between decoder queries and encoder keys
Apply softmax to get attention weights
Compute a weighted sum of the values using the attention weights
questions.explanation: Cross-attention starts with the encoder providing keys and values (its stored representations). The decoder generates queries (what it needs). Attention scores are computed between queries and keys, softmax normalizes them into weights, and a weighted sum of values produces the context that informs the decoder's next output.

questions.question: Order the sub-layers within a single Transformer decoder block.
items: Feed-forward network
Cross-attention to encoder output
Masked self-attention over previously generated tokens
correct_order: Masked self-attention over previously generated tokens
Cross-attention to encoder output
Feed-forward network
questions.explanation: Each decoder block first performs masked self-attention over previously generated tokens (ensuring the model only sees past outputs). Then cross-attention lets it consult the encoder's representations. Finally, a feed-forward network processes each position independently. Residual connections and layer normalization are applied after each sub-layer.

questions.question: Order these Transformer architecture types from most to least suited for text classification.
items: Decoder-only (GPT)
Encoder-decoder (T5)
Encoder-only (BERT)
correct_order: Encoder-only (BERT)
Encoder-decoder (T5)
Decoder-only (GPT)
questions.explanation: Encoder-only models like BERT are best for classification because bidirectional attention builds the richest representations, and the [CLS] token provides a ready-made sequence summary. Encoder-decoder models can classify but are over-engineered for it. Decoder-only models are least suited because their one-directional representations miss right-side context.

questions.question: Order these steps in how an encoder-decoder model performs summarization.
items: The decoder generates a summary token by token
The encoder reads the full article with bidirectional attention
The decoder uses cross-attention to focus on relevant parts of the article
The input article is tokenized and given positional encodings
The final summary is produced when the model outputs a stop token
correct_order: The input article is tokenized and given positional encodings
The encoder reads the full article with bidirectional attention
The decoder uses cross-attention to focus on relevant parts of the article
The decoder generates a summary token by token
The final summary is produced when the model outputs a stop token
questions.explanation: Summarization starts with tokenizing and adding positional encodings to the article. The encoder processes it bidirectionally to build a rich understanding. The decoder then uses cross-attention to focus on relevant parts while generating the summary one token at a time, stopping when it produces a stop/end token.
